title: Why UX Research Is the Foundation of Every Great Design
excerpt: Before jumping into wireframes or Figma, the real work starts with understanding the people who will use your product. Here's how I approach research in every project.
content:
Every project I take on starts the same way — not with sketches or color palettes, but with questions. Who are we building this for? What problems do they face? What does success look like for them?

UX research isn't a phase you complete and forget. It's the backbone that supports every decision you make throughout the design process. Without it, you're just guessing — and guessing is expensive.

My Research Toolkit

Depending on the project, I combine different methods to build a clear picture of the user's world:

User Interviews: One-on-one conversations are invaluable. I typically conduct 5–8 interviews at the start of a project. The goal isn't to validate assumptions — it's to discover things I didn't know I didn't know.

Competitive Audits: I look at how similar products solve the same problems. Not to copy, but to understand what users are already trained to expect and where existing solutions fall short.

Surveys & Questionnaires: When I need quantitative data to complement qualitative insights, short surveys help me identify patterns across a larger group.

Turning Research Into Action

Raw research data is useless if it stays in a spreadsheet. I synthesize findings into personas, journey maps, and opportunity statements that the entire team can reference.

The most important outcome of research isn't a deliverable — it's shared understanding. When designers, developers, and stakeholders all operate from the same insights, the product practically designs itself.

A Real Example

On a recent SaaS dashboard project, initial stakeholder interviews pointed toward a feature-heavy interface. But after interviewing actual users, I discovered that 80% of them only used three core features daily. That insight completely reshaped the information architecture and simplified the UI by removing unnecessary complexity.

That's the power of research. It replaces opinion with evidence, and the end result is always a better experience.
